posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked: I’ve recently been sold on the idea of exploring Muscadet. This is a type of wine with which I have exactly zero experience. Where should I start with this wine, and where will it lead me? Are there different styles of Muscadet? Which producers and vintages should I seek-out? TIA.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Ɠřәǥǥ Ɠřәәŋþąųӎ

Pepiere (Marc Oliver)
Luneau-Papin
Bregeon
Comerais

posts.name: Benjamin Sullivan DNA
posts.cooked:
G. Greenbaum:

Pepiere (Marc Oliver)

  • 1,000,000,000

I’m no expert, but have had quite a few of these, particularly from the producers that Trip-G mentions. These can age amazingly well, too, for wines that seem to be stylistically on the light side. I’ve been drinking some 2000 and 2002 that have been just delicious, deep and complex. Having said that, you will likely find these to be more on the mineral end of the scale, more acid, not at all like SB or Chard, but more like a toned down white Rhone. Think sparkling wine w/o the bubbles for an idea of the weight, not taste.

You will hear to drink them with raw oysters and other seafood, and that’s a good choice, but it’s a pretty versatile wine.

posts.name: Joe Hauck
posts.cooked:
Benjamin Sullivan:
G. Greenbaum:

Pepiere (Marc Oliver)

  • 1,000,000,000
  • 2,000,000,000 Pepiere “Clos des Briords” is all you need to know.

posts.name: Rick Smith
posts.cooked:
Joe Hauck:
Benjamin Sullivan:
G. Greenbaum:

Pepiere (Marc Oliver)

  • 1,000,000,000
  • 2,000,000,000 Pepiere “Clos des Briords” is all you need to know.

En magnum

Brian - it’s often a rather light, acidic wine. As a grape, Melon doesn’t have a hell of a lot of personality, very much like Chardonnay, which is a sibling. Generally where it’s grown on the west end of the Loire, it’s pretty cool and damp with the Atlantic weather coming in. So it rarely gets really ripe and sweet. You end up with a grape that’s characteristic of very cool-climate viticulture. Unlike say Riesling, however, which has very identifiable aromas and flavors, Melon is more neutral.

Two kind of cool things to know - first, Muscadet is unusual in that it’s not the name of the region or the grape, so in that sense it’s kind of an anomaly in France. Second, supposedly the region was first planted at the direction of the emperor Probus. He was originally from a wine making family in Pannonia, which is now in Hungary, where he owned property and had his soldiers plant as well. Tokaj is a bit farther east but he was there too and when I’m trying to describe a dry Furmint to people, the closest analogy I can come up with is Muscadet. The vine isn’t similar, nor is the grape, but the resulting wine is usually a very dry, acidic, citrusy wine that is often modified greatly by the tricks of the winemaker. So at either end of the empire, somewhat similar grapes were planted by the same guy.

Anyhow, that’s a stretch so back to the grape. Because it’s very light and almost insipid, people do a lot to it. The neighboring regions have plenty of Sauvignon Blanc with it’s characteristic bell-pepper and grapefruit notes. Nothing quite as identifiable with the Muscadet. So one thing people do is age it on the lees. If you find a Muscadet sur lie, it’s supposed to have been on the lees for the entire winter. Some people keep it on the lees even longer. That supposedly gives it a bigger, creamier mouthfeel, which is why they do it with Chardonnay as well, but the funny thing is, you still have a pretty dry, acidic wine and I’d guess that many people would be hard-pressed to differentiate blind. However, since the sur lie wines are still inexpensive, buy both and find out.

There are subregions, for example, Muscadet-Sèvre et Maine, which might be the best known, but the entire region is not a huge area to begin with and I’m not certain that there’s all that much difference between the subregions. I’d think there’s more dif between producers.

Sometime in the 1990s they brought in stainless steel and most of the wines done today, including those suggested above, are done in stainless steel, which was of course a concept introduced from California. They used to do the wine in wood or concrete and some people are still doing that, but in general there’s nothing “traditional” about the wines that are most prized today and to me, that’s a good thing because a lot of them are really good, generally quite clean, and thoroughly enjoyable when you’re in the mood for that style. It’s a little amazing but you can find some excellent wines for really low prices and for me, the wines from the Loire in general remain some of the best values out there. Generally as a profile you have fairly muted aromas, sometimes with some tropical fruit or grassy notes, and on the palate you have lots of green apple and lemon acidity that leaves a nice clean finish. Cheers.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
Thank you for chiming-in, guys.

Greg,
Huge thanks to you — your post multiplied my knowledge of Muscadet by a factor larger than I can quickly figure-out!

earlier today I bought the only Muscadet carried by my local wine retailer: 2009 Muscadet Sevre et Maine Selection des Hauts Pemions Sur Lie
The shop owner told me it is a good example of Muscadet, and is “what Muscadet should be” considering its humble price (I only had to fork out about $10 for it).

I’m looking to expand my knowledge of white wines, as I find myself increasingly favoring them over reds, as I find them more enjoyable to drink with food, and it’s usually the food I’m eating that dictates which wine I open. I’ve heard good things about Muscadet here-and-there on this board and others, and a particular person recenlty convinced me I need to explore these wines, so now I’m motivated.

Because they are in-season right now, I plan on trying it out with a bowl full of mussels.

Keep the recs. coming, folks. I appreciate the guidance.

  • BG

Gregg is correct:
Pepiere

posts.name: Rick Gregory
posts.cooked:
I love Pepeire… but this is actually a region with a lot of good producers and it’s hard to go wrong. Example: I grabbed an $11 bottles of Domaine de la Quilla Sevre et Maine from 2010. On opening it was a bit closed, maybe slightly reductive. It opened nicely the next night. Then I forgot about it… for a week. Grabbed the rest of the bottle last night and it was great, with pure pear flavors. After a week on the countertop.

Honestly, I’d buy a mixed case and drink through it. The 2010s are quite nice, they’re cheap and you’ll spend maybe $150 for the case.

Note that they DO age. For decades if stored properly. Which reminds me, I have some from the mid90s to check in on…

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
My two favorites:

Luneau-Lapin L d’Or. The grapes are grown on gneiss, granite, and mica. This has to be one of my favorite cuvees, and it ages. A 1989 I had absolutely sang.

Pepiere Clos de Briords. Here, the soils are clay and sand with a granite subsoil. An incredibly elegant and complex wine that can be had for $15. I can think of no greater value out there.

posts.name: Jim Karegeannes
posts.cooked:
The wines from Delhommeau have become our summer house whites, his Cuvée Harmonie in particular.
Just picked up a new one (to me) his Cuvée Nathalie. Looking forward to trying.

posts.name: Jim Friedman
posts.cooked: Try Pepiere Granite de Clisson. 2005 and 2007 were both great wines.

posts.name: Nigel Groundwater
posts.cooked:
My favourite Muscadets come from Marc Ollivier [Domaine de la Pepiere], Pierre Luneau-Papin and now his son Pierre-Marie Luneau [Domaine Pierre de la Grange] which have already been mentioned PLUS Guy Bossard [Domaine de L’Ecu]

Ollivier’s wines have been difficult to find in the UK with the majority of his production going to the USA and I have mostly had his various cuvees in France although some are now available here. I have recently bought the excellent 2009 Clos des Briords and Les Gras Moutons including the odd old vintage of the former.

Pierre Luneau-Papin’s cuvees are more widely available at least in the UK [e.g. Le L d’Or 2009 is and will be great but the 1999 can also still be found] as are Guy Bossard’s biodynamic wines.

I have not tasted Bossard’s latest vintages but I am currently drinking his 2005 and 2006 Granite, Gneiss and Orthogneiss cuvees which IMO are very worthy though less expensive [they are all relatively inexpensive] comparisons with the Ollivier and Luneau-Papin wines. The 2005 Granite [which was also issued under the Les Amis Vignerons d’Anne-Claude Leflaive label – no bad endorsement in itself] has been particularly good.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
I’d just echo all of the recommendations here, and (of course) especially the Pepiere. Others seem good, and variety is laudable, but the Pepiere wines always seem a bit better than most others, and at no more money. I do need to explore a bit more myself, I particularly need to try L d’Or, as I hear good things.

It’s only in the past year or so that I’ve come to realize what a wonderful region the Loire is for a vast diversity of excellent wines. From the wonderful whites of muscadet a good next stop are Chenins from further upriver. Remarkable wines, and if not quite as inexpensive as muscadet, the value is still incredible. Then of course there are all of the superb red wines that the region has to offer. For my taste, if you find yourself more drawn to the refreshing and cleansingly food-friendly aspects of whites, you may really enjoy a lot of Loire reds. To me Loire Cab Franc, Gamay, and even Cot offer wines of depth and substance that tend to remain fresh and crisp in all but the ripest years or treatments.

posts.name: Ɠřәǥǥ Ɠřәәŋþąųӎ
posts.cooked: No, you’ve got it wrong. Nothing in the Loire to see…move along

posts.name: RS Beck
posts.cooked:
I’ve had excellent experiences with these producers.

Pepiere (Briords)

Bregeon

Delhommeau (Harmonie)

Brian - I forgot to mention, as Rick says, those wines can age. That’s something that’s even more amazing when you consider the prices. I have some from the mid 1990s and they’re just amazing. I tend to blow thru them pretty quickly because they’re so inexpensive but unintentionally I kept a few for a while and when I opened them I was very pleasantly surprised. Now I don’t worry about not getting to them. They retain that acidity but they take on the interesting kind of nutty notes of aged whites on top. I have a few from 1997 and my plan was to try them on the 15 year mark. No particular reason other than it seemed like a nice round number.

BTW - I should say that you might take a look at some reds too. If you’re not interested in the style, that’s understandable and many people aren’t. But again, they have their place and in the right context, can be pretty wonderful and even better, can be real bargains. There’s a co-op producer in Saumur that puts out both a white and a red, both retailing for under $10 most places. I can testify that the red ages and actually IMPROVES for at least 8 years. What a fantastic buy. If I’m not mistaken, the white is Chenin Blanc and that’s another white you might consider exploring. It has more native personality than Melon but has a similar family style if you will - usually very bright and crisp, w/out a lot of weight. Good stuff.

It’s because I enjoyed those so much that a few years ago I started looking at other whites that I’d not really paid much attention to before. Spain I kind of knew but Italy and Greece are two places that I started exploring. Lots to learn!

posts.name: Rick Gregory
posts.cooked: Speaking of reds, Marc Ollivier also makes a Cot that’s aromatically interesting light, translucent and just FUN. It’s about $15. But… Cot = Malbec. I was floored when I realized this the first time. His Cab Franc is fun too, but the idea that Malbec could make a wine like that blew my mind.

Jim Karegeannes:

The wines from Delhommeau have become our summer house whites…
Just picked up a new one (to me) his Cuvée Nathalie. Looking forward to trying.

Drank this tonight–very nice, and I wish I’d bought a couple more.

posts.name: Todd Hamina
posts.cooked: All great suggestions and if you are an oyster fan your life has just improved dramatically. If not, scallops could be your new best friend, or salmon. MdB is an interesting grape to work with (I’ve been making it since 07). It ripens unevenly on the vine, having some bright green clusters next to more golden and translucent ones. The very ripest of all are speckled with brown spots and may be at 30 brix whereas the the bright green ones are at 17. So I usually just wait and wait, then pick on the impending doom of rain. The bottom line is to drink through a slew of producers and always bear in mind that it is up to you to figure out the food aspect because this grape shines on the dinner table. Have fun.

The 2005 Granite [which was also issued under the Les Amis Vignerons d’Anne-Claude Leflaive label – no bad endorsement in itself] has been particularly good.

posts.name: chaad thomas
posts.cooked:
Just a couple of thoughts, mostly in response to Greg’s post:

  1. I thought Melon was related to Gamay?

  2. I also thought Muscadet was the local name for Melon, and the French seem to use the word that way. If not, what is Muscadet?

  3. I have found that the sur-lie wines have a textural density that the basic stuff does not, and which really gives the wines some interest.

  4. Metaireau “Carte Noir” is a great example of the sur lie style

user_status.description: Hosting The Real WIne Show podcast!

posts.cooked:
Chaad - sigh.

It is a parent of Gamay as well as Chardonnay and Riesling and Romarantin and many other grapes. I never heard of most of them but those stick out.

And it brings us back to our friend the Emperor Probus. I remember years ago reading Gibbons and this guy comes into the narrative. One of the bright lights in the long decline. Anyhow, he made his rep fighting off the Germanic invaders in Gaul - people like the Franks, the Burgundians, the Alemani, and others. And he had the brilliant insight that an idle army is a bad thing. Hell, they might even rise up and kill you. In fact, that’s what they finally did, but before that he kept his guys working, building roads and fortifications, and planting vineyards.

Now remember that grapes were introduced to Spain and Italy by the Greeks and Phoenicians, but Romans took the grapes to the interior of Europe. There weren’t grapes in Gaul until the Roman times but once they came in, the people there started planting all over. Since that destroyed the export market for Rome, grape planting outside of Italy was banned. Probus realized that was dumb. He rescinded the rule and had his guys plant all over the place. After all, there were only so many roads that needed work, and they could make wine from their vineyards after planting. But he was from the east. Dalmatia actually, directly south of Pannonia. So what grapes did he introduce to Germany and France? Grapes from his homeland.

One of the reasons Attila liked Pannonia so much was that it was fertile, good for growing things like grapes. His legacy stuck and they eventually called all of the grapes from the east the “Hunnish” grapes. Those already in Gaul were called the “Frankish” grapes. So today in Austria and Hungary we have Blaufrankisch, which only means the black grape from the Franks. Even though it’s not grown in France or Germany, one would imagine that’s where it came from.

Probus was introducing grapes from his homeland but he didn’t call them “Hunnish” at the time because he lived a few hundred years before Attila came along. Anyhow, later those grapes introduced by him became known as the Hunnish grapes in Germany and in France. I don’t think he introduced Gouais Blanc to the Loire directly - he was fighting Germanic invaders and most likely had his soldiers plant it up there during idle times as he didn’t have any real military pressure that required his attention in the Loire region.

Here’s a pretty cool map that shows the various regions we’re discussing.

http://www.bible-history.com/maps/romanempire/Pannonia.html

It does make sense that Gouais Blanc, which was considered “hunnish” in the Middle Ages, would be a parent of Riesling though, and a parent of Chardonnay, and of Romarantin, since those came from regions in which Probus was fighting. It also makes sense that since it comes from a very different lineage than the grapes in the west, when mixed with those varieties, it would produce a vigorous bunch of offspring.

As an aside, when the Central and Eastern European countries joined the EU, France had seen to it that there was a rule in place prohibiting new vineyard acreage. You could only plant what had historically been planted, or the equivalent acreage if you didn’t plant the historical sites. So the wise-ass Hungarians promptly produced maps showing that half the country had been planted at some time. Good old Probus has some responsibility for that.

Anyhow, as far as the name Muscadet - that is sometimes used as the name of the grape too these days. But it’s really Melon de Bourgogne, which would indicate that it originated somewhere in Burgundy. Cheers!

posts.name: Steven Heydemann
posts.cooked: There’s a very easy way to get up to speed on Muscadet – one of my favorite whites for its stone-salt-mineral-acid quality, its longevity, and its price. Get in touch with Chambers Street Wine and let them put a mixed case together for you. You’ll be very well-served if you do.

posts.name: Nigel Groundwater
posts.cooked:
Interesting re grape genetics and the role of the Emperor Probus in the spread of grapes and the apparently very extensive role of the [humble] Gouais Blanc has in the family trees of many major grapes including Chardonnay and Riesling.

However the relationship of Gamay [Noir and Blanc] to Melon is more than just Gouais Blanc since they apparently [as with Chardonnay ] also share the other parent, Pinot.
For some of their siblings whether the ‘other’ parent was Pinot Noir, Blanc or Gris is unkown because Pinot Noir is heterozygous for berry colour but for e.g. Gamay Noir it will have been Pinot Noir and because it has always been the most common form of ‘Pinot’ it has been considered to be the most probable ‘other’ parent for the siblings.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
Todd Hamina:

All great suggestions and if you are an oyster fan your life has just improved dramatically. If not, scallops could be your new best friend, or salmon. MdB is an interesting grape to work with (I’ve been making it since 07). It ripens unevenly on the vine, having some bright green clusters next to more golden and translucent ones. The very ripest of all are speckled with brown spots and may be at 30 brix whereas the the bright green ones are at 17. So I usually just wait and wait, then pick on the impending doom of rain. The bottom line is to drink through a slew of producers and always bear in mind that it is up to you to figure out the food aspect because this grape shines on the dinner table. Have fun.

Just realized a couple weekends ago that I absolutely love oysters. Already knew I love scallops, and I like salmon.

So, with deciding when to pick: is the idea behind picking late that the super-ripe grapes will balance-out the tart underripe grapes? very interesting.

Todd Hamina:

All great suggestions and if you are an oyster fan your life has just improved dramatically. If not, scallops could be your new best friend, or salmon. MdB is an interesting grape to work with (I’ve been making it since 07). It ripens unevenly on the vine, having some bright green clusters next to more golden and translucent ones. The very ripest of all are speckled with brown spots and may be at 30 brix whereas the the bright green ones are at 17. So I usually just wait and wait, then pick on the impending doom of rain. The bottom line is to drink through a slew of producers and always bear in mind that it is up to you to figure out the food aspect because this grape shines on the dinner table. Have fun.

Just realized a couple weekends ago that I absolutely love oysters. Already knew I love scallops, and I like salmon.

So, with deciding when to pick: is the idea behind picking late that the super-ripe grapes will balance-out the tart underripe grapes? very interesting.

You are going to be a happy man. And yes, it is a question of balance. The high acid grapes provide that great framework and the over ripe, the delicious nose. Sur lie aging offers a tremendous mouthfeel and when they all intermingle well something beautiful occurs. The fact that they are so delicious at such resonable pricing makes them a no brainer, especially if you’re into shellfish. Triple cream cheeses are also a nice foil.

Drink everything and report back, cheers!

Now, I just have to find a retailer near me so I can start trying them now!

posts.name: Nigel Groundwater
posts.cooked:
Brian G r a f s t r o m:

Hmmm, ok. Good to know. Is it Huet that makes a vouvray that’s really expensive? … I must be thinking of someone else.

The great sweet Chenin Blancs [and Vouvray producers figure heavily including Huet] are amongst the more expensive Loire wines.

For example Huet’s Cuvee Constance may be one of the wines you were thinking of at e.g. £250 for the 89 and £65 for the 50cl 09.

OTOH Loire wines [red and white] are usually much less expensive than ‘equivalent’ Bordeaux, Burgundy and even Rhone wines.

Brian G r a f s t r o m:
T. Gadd:

Huet make $30 wine, and also $150 for a half wine. If you’re exploring the Loire and looking to the high end, I’d check out Didier Dagueneau before, but it isn’t Chenin!

I’ve had one Dagueneau — the '06 Pur Sang, iirc — it was kindly opened by a friend of mine at a going away party for Ashley and I before we headed over to England. It was the best Sancerre I’ve ever had. I’ve been trying to duplicate that experience with Cotat’s lineup, but have been unable to do so. Cotat’s wines are very good, though.

Since the Pur Sang is not Sancerre, it will be hard to duplicate with Sancerre.

But it would also be hard to duplicate with Pouilly-Fumé. Despite being from the appellation, Dagueneau’s wines are not - at least to my palate - Pouilly-Fumé. They’re atypical. They’re sui generis. They’re Vin de Dagueneau.

For an excellent Pouilly-Fumé that is typical of the appellation, try Cuvée Silice from Francis Blanchet.

I’ve had one Dagueneau — the '06 Pur Sang, iirc — it was kindly opened by a friend of mine at a going away party for Ashley and I before we headed over to England. It was the best Sancerre I’ve ever had. I’ve been trying to duplicate that experience with Cotat’s lineup, but have been unable to do so. Cotat’s wines are very good, though.

Since the Pur Sang is not Sancerre, it will be hard to duplicate with Sancerre.

But it would also be hard to duplicate with Pouilly-Fumé. Despite being from the appellation, Dagueneau’s wines are not - at least to my palate - Pouilly-Fumé. They’re atypical. They’re sui generis. They’re Vin de Dagueneau.

For an excellent Pouilly-Fumé that is typical of the appellation, try Cuvée Silice from Francis Blanchet.

OTOH Didier Dagueneau made [and I assume the Domaine run by his children still do] a pretty exceptional Sancerre latterly from his own small vineyard on the Monts Damnes, Chavignol which is also in a Dagueneau style which is different from that of the Cotat cousins Pascal and Francois - which are excellent in their own right and IMO not very typical [of Sancerre] either.

AFAIK it was Edmond Vatan who encouraged Dagueneau to cross the river and the Vatan’s Clos la Neore Sancerre [now made by his daughter Anne] is also a treat worth seeking out. Like Dagueneau’s wines its excellence has made it expensive.

posts.cooked:

It was the best Sancerre I’ve ever had. I’ve been trying to duplicate that experience with Cotat’s lineup, but have been unable to do so. Cotat’s wines are very good, though.

• 2014 Domaine de la Pépière (Marc Ollivier) Muscadet de Sèvre-et-Maine Sur Lie Vieilles Vignes Clos des Briords _- France, Loire Valley, Pays Nantais, Muscadet de Sèvre-et-Maine (12/6/2015)
    Love me some melon de Bourgogne.
    _
    The wine is light yellow color. Sweet nose. Bright acid, lime, lemon rind and mouthwatering. I tried the '12 vintage a few months ago and was taken aback by the level of complexity. This vintage shows all the same characteristics. An amazing QPR for an extremely high quality wine. I bought 5 bottles and then let Sean take two of them when he inquired, share and share alike.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
G. Greenbaum:

Pepiere (Marc Oliver)
Luneau-Papin

Several other good producers have been suggested, but in my opinion, these are the greats, and no one else. In particular, Pepiere Clisson and several bottlings from Luneau-Papin (occasionally I’ve seen library releases of Cuvee L D’Or, which ages very slowly) are the best I’ve had.

As far as the Chenin talk, Huet’s wines other than the Cuvee Constance are not expensive, and they are amazing values other than possibly the 2012s and 2013s. Chidaine makes great wines every year, and extremely reasonably priced. 2014 is a great vintage for exploring Loire Chenin. I don’t even pay attention to vintage quality for Chidaine, though. Some are better than others, but all are very, very good.

I disagree that Didier Dagueneau’s wines are not typical; they’re just better than everyone else’s in Pouilly-Fume and Sancerre. 2007 is the last vintage he saw through basically to completion, and in my opinion, it’s the last consistently great vintage. I have seen experienced tasters get them down to Sancerre or Pouilly Fume (and telling those apart is basically impossible, despite what some of the books say, unless you are unbelievably experienced with specific producers) in double blind tastings. If that doesn’t demonstrate typicity, I don’t know what does.

There’s my opinionated take on the discussion(s).

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Getting to this a bit late already, but you’ve seen the pattern Brian—Pepiere and Luneau-Papin. I really like the value Muscadet delivers for me and have had some that have aged beautifully–a 2001 L D’Or drunk 2 years ago comes to mind right away.

I’ve tried the Granite de Clissons and it was good, but I like the de L’or and Clos des Briords a bit better.

Bob is right about Dagueneau—absolutely singular producer, and vins de Dagueneau fits. My only comment is that so far, Serge is not Didier, unfortunately. I would love to have a recent example that would disprove that statement. I really like Cotat (both brothers).

For Loire Chenin, just go over and raid FMIII’s fridge

Mike

Mike

Unfortunately, that’s been my experience as well.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
Doug Schulman:
G. Greenbaum:

Pepiere (Marc Oliver)
Luneau-Papin

Several other good producers have been suggested, but in my opinion, these are the greats, and no one else. In particular, Pepiere Clisson and several bottlings from Luneau-Papin (occasionally I’ve seen library releases of Cuvee L D’Or, which ages very slowly) are the best I’ve had.

As far as the Chenin talk, Huet’s wines other than the Cuvee Constance are not expensive, and they are amazing values other than possibly the 2012s and 2013s. Chidaine makes great wines every year, and extremely reasonably priced. 2014 is a great vintage for exploring Loire Chenin. I don’t even pay attention to vintage quality for Chidaine, though. Some are better than others, but all are very, very good.

There’s my opinionated take on the discussion(s).

Lately I prefer Jo Landron to Luneau-Papin. I find his wines (and Olivier’s Pepiere) to have more tension than Luneau-Papin, for which the entire range seems rounder than its Pepiere equivalents.

Brian you should not find Chenin flat at all. Chenin is perhaps unique (in the true sense) amongst common grapes for its extremely high levels of both sugars, and thus potential alcohol, and acidity. Huet from a good vintage should be electric in its acidic vibrancy. Huet is the standard-bearer and while perhaps not “expensive” they tend to run a bit higher than most. Chidaine a step behind, Pinon a step behind, etc. Some others are as much or more but I don’t explore much beyond these names.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
Yes, over the past four years I’ve seen the light w/r/t Chenin. The first few examples I ever tried simply weren’t very good, in retrospect.

It’s nice to see this thread revived. I actually do have a bottle each of Luneau-Papin and Pepiere at home, but I’ve still not yet tried a wine from either producer. The time is near!

A local retailer recently blew through a bunch of 2006 Morandiere Muscadet for $20/bottle. I was able to grab a few, and it is a ridiculous QPR at that price.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: When people say that Serge isn’t Didier, do they mean that Louis Benjamin (Didier’s son) isn’t Didier, or are they actually referring to the Serge Daguenau estate, which is a different domaine entirely as far as I know?

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Michael Powers:

Lately I prefer Jo Landron to Luneau-Papin. I find his wines (and Olivier’s Pepiere) to have more tension than Luneau-Papin, for which the entire range seems rounder than its Pepiere equivalents.

Brian you should not find Chenin flat at all. Chenin is perhaps unique (in the true sense) amongst common grapes for its extremely high levels of both sugars, and thus potential alcohol, and acidity. Huet from a good vintage should be electric in its acidic vibrancy. Huet is the standard-bearer and while perhaps not “expensive” they tend to run a bit higher than most. Chidaine a step behind, Pinon a step behind, etc. Some others are as much or more but I don’t explore much beyond these names.

I don’t find Chidaine a step behind Huet, especially recently. Some of Chidaine’s '09s and '10s impressed the heck out of me, and he’s done really well with some difficult vintages since then where Huet seems to have faltered. Either way, I think most, if not all, wines from both producers are really well valued, sometimes ridiculously so.

I can understand preferring Landron’s wines to those of Luneau-Papin, and I agree with the distinction you make in style. I think L-P’s wines are more consistently in the upper quality tier that Muscadet can achieve, but they both make some really good wines.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

When people say that Serge isn’t Didier, do they mean that Louis Benjamin (Didier’s son) isn’t Didier, or are they actually referring to the Serge Daguenau estate, which is a different domaine entirely as far as I know?

Sorry, I didn’t know his name and agreed with what I saw. I did mean his son, Louis Benjamin. I am sure doing things completely on his own is a lot different than having Didier there, so hopefully he’s on his way to making wines just as great as earlier vintages produced.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Sorry to be late to the thread but all I can add is that I buy almost all the M. Ollivier bottlings from the Pepiere to the Clisson with the exception of the Trois and Quatre which (unlike several of my friends) I don’t like as much.

Often buy the Luneau Papin L d’Or as well.

But that doesn’t mean there aren’t a number of other excellent Muscadet producers. I’d buy with confidence pretty much anything Chambers St. is selling. David Lillie knows his Muscadet.

I’ll also add you’re getting in at a good time. 2014 is a top vintage.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Might be worth tasting the Serge Dagueneau wines too. Rather in the shadow of the late, great Didier, but I’ve had a smattering of pretty compelling bottles. They have some old vines and seem to work very well.
